title: GitHub for Collaboration
text:
Meet the lecturer
Lars Schöbitz


Environmental Engineer 
Retired researcher 
RStudio certified instructor
Data steward at ETHZ

title: GitHub for Collaboration
section: What this day is, and is not
text:
What this day is, and is not

Today is the Git and GitHub foundation for collaboration: create, clone, commit, push, branch, pull request, review, merge.
Today is not an AI workshop. Working with AI coding agents builds on exactly these skills; that is the follow-up workshop.
It is safe to fail here. Everything we do today is reversible, and breaking things is part of the plan.


Defuse the AI-agent expectation gap now, in minute 5, not in minute 90. Say the safe-to-fail promise out loud; it comes back in B3 with the revert demo.

title: GitHub for Collaboration
text:
Learning objectives
By the end of this workshop, participants will be able to:

Create and clone a repository, and run the full pull, stage, commit, push cycle from the RStudio IDE, including reverting a commit.
Work with branches and pull requests: create a branch, open a pull request, review a colleague’s pull request, and merge after review.
Publish a rendered document via GitHub Pages, and draw a concept map of how Git and GitHub fit together.

title: GitHub for Collaboration
text:
Teaching strategy

Teaching Git on a “need to know” basis
Teaching only a very small subset of Git’s functionality
Creating a safe space for learning and getting support
Focus on collaboration aspects of GitHub
Rhythm in every block: short slides, my turn (watch), your turn (do)

title: GitHub for Collaboration
text:
Schedule









Time
Module

Arrival and setup check


10:00 - 10:30
B1: Welcome, concept-map intro, baseline map


10:30 - 11:00
B2: Create and clone (website repo)


11:00 - 11:15
Break


11:15 - 11:45
B3: The commit cycle (website repo)


11:45 - 12:15
B4: Collaboration cliffhanger (partner clone, push fails)


12:15 - 13:00
Lunch


13:00 - 13:30
B5: Branch (manuscript repo)


13:30 - 14:05
B6: Pull request, review, merge


14:05 - 14:15

14:15 - 14:30
B8: Concept map revisit and wrap-up

title: GitHub for Collaboration
text:
Illustrations from the Openscapes blog GitHub for supporting, contributing, and failing safely by Allison Horst and Julia Lowndes


Why GitHub?
“Collaboration is the most compelling reason to manage a project with Git and GitHub. My definition of collaboration includes hands-on participation by multiple people, including your past and future self, as well as an asymmetric model, in which some people are active makers and others only read or review.” - Jenny Bryan
Bryan J. 2017. Excuse me, do you have a moment to talk about version control? PeerJ Preprints 5:e3159v2 https://doi.org/10.7287/peerj.preprints.3159v2

title: GitHub for Collaboration
text:
Illustrations from the Openscapes blog GitHub for supporting, contributing, and failing safely by Allison Horst and Julia Lowndes


GitHub helps us fail safely. Because we can return to previous versions easily, painful consequences of failing are reduced. We can bravely try things knowing that some will fail, and that failing is not catastrophic: we start again from where we left off, with a clear history to learn from.
This is the safe-to-fail promise from the expectations slide, now with a picture.

title: GitHub for Collaboration
text:
Illustrations from the Openscapes blog GitHub for supporting, contributing, and failing safely by Allison Horst and Julia Lowndes


GitHub streamlines our work: it takes care of otherwise time-consuming bookkeeping and unrelenting file tracking. Instead of fretting over whether “analysis_final_v2.xls” or “analysis_final_final.xls” is truly the final version, we can be confident in the completed analysis and move on. This connects directly to what learners draw in the baseline map in a few minutes: email attachments and file naming.

title: GitHub for Collaboration
text:
Illustrations from the Openscapes blog GitHub for supporting, contributing, and failing safely by Allison Horst and Julia Lowndes


Once we are empowered to work collaboratively with GitHub, a whole new world of projects opens up, because we can contribute in many different ways. This transferrable ability to collaborate might just be the best thing about GitHub. Keep this to one breath; the motivation section ends here at about minute 10.

title: GitHub for Collaboration
text:
Concept maps
A concept map is things (nodes) joined by labelled arrows, where every label is a verb.

Read an arrow out loud and it becomes a sentence: “the kettle HEATS the water.”

Per Greg Wilson, Teaching Tech Together: concepts as nodes, labelled relationships as edges. Not a flowchart, not a timeline, not a list; no start, no end.
One sentence on why: experts use concept maps to make their mental models visible. Yours today will show how you currently think documents travel, and this evening the second map will show what changed.
8-10 minutes for this intro including the exemplar walk-through.

title: GitHub for Collaboration
text:
Your turn: your baseline map

How does a document you write reach your co-author and come back? Draw it as a concept map.

Things as nodes, arrows with verb labels.
There is no wrong map; this one is yours.
Keep the map. You will need it at 14:15.


Place the yellow sticky note on your laptop when your map is done.








One single prompt, not a fused double question. Expected content: email attachments, Dropbox, Google Docs, file naming (final_v2, final_final). That is the point; the day connects Git to these habits. Do not correct anything here.
Bounded regroup: 2 minutes, then park-and-pair; 1:1 fix at the next break.

title: GitHub for Collaboration
text:
Live drawing: local and remote
Projector goes to sleep. We draw this one by hand.

Live drawing 1 (local and remote): two boxes, clone first, then push and pull. Under 3 minutes. Fallback: the export in the appendix of the handbook, images/drawing-1-local-remote.png.

title: GitHub for Collaboration
text:
My turn

Watch first; your turn comes next. Hands off the keyboards.

On github.com: create a new repository called website, public, with a README.
In RStudio: two settings that save future pain (Tools > Global Options): never save .RData on exit, and use the native pipe |>.
Clone: green “Code” button, copy the HTTPS URL, then File > New Project > Version Control > Git.
Inspect what arrived: the Files tab, the Git tab, and two new files with yellow ? icons: .gitignore and website.Rproj.


Follow along on the screen.



Demo script, letter by letter as rehearsed:

Create the repo on GitHub with README so the clone is never empty.
RStudio settings: Tools > Global Options > General: Save workspace to .RData on exit: Never. Tools > Global Options > Code: check use native pipe operator.
Clone into the pre-work folder structure (~/Documents/gitrepos/gh-org-gitforsci-ghe is the example from the setup test; for their own repos, gh-USERNAME).
The .Rproj and .gitignore explanation lives here in the notes, not on a slide: .Rproj holds RStudio project settings, .gitignore lists what Git should not track; the yellow ? icons mean Git sees them but nobody has told it what to do with them yet. This answers the cis learner question “why do .Rproj session files show up?”. Mention hidden files exist (files starting with a dot are hidden by the operating system).

title: GitHub for Collaboration
text:
Your turn


On github.com: create a repository called website, public, with a README.
In RStudio: File > New Project > Version Control > Git, paste the HTTPS URL, create the project inside your gitrepos folder.
Find .gitignore and website.Rproj in the Git tab with their yellow ? icons.


Place the yellow sticky note on your laptop when you see the two yellow ? icons.








Bounded regroup: 2 minutes, then park-and-pair; 1:1 fix at the next break.

title: GitHub for Collaboration
text:
Take a break
Please get up and move! Leave your emails alone. We continue at 11:15.







Image generated with DALL-E 3 by OpenAI

title: GitHub for Collaboration
text:
Live drawing: the commit cycle
Projector goes to sleep. We draw this one by hand.

Live drawing 2 (commit cycle): working files, stage, commit as labelled snapshot on the history line, push. Leave room for the history line to grow. Under 3 minutes. Fallback: images/drawing-2-commit-cycle.png.

title: GitHub for Collaboration
text:
My turn

Watch first; your turn comes next.

Edit README.md: add the line “This repo contains a personal website.” Save, and spot the blue M in the Git tab.
The cycle, always in this order: Pull, Stage, Commit, Push.
Look at the history: the commit is a labelled snapshot, on GitHub too.
Now on purpose: one bad commit, and one revert.


Follow along on the screen.



Demo script:

Edit README, save, point out the blue M next to the file in the Git tab.
Sync in 4 steps, said as a mantra: Pull (nothing to get, but build the habit), Stage (checkbox), Commit (message: “describe the repo content”), Push. Then open the repo on GitHub and show the commit message and the file change there.
Revert demo, instructor only: make a deliberately bad commit (for example delete half the README), commit it, then revert it via the history; show that the history keeps both the mistake and the undo. Say the safety-net message out loud: almost everything in Git is reversible. This answers the recorded cis question “how do I take a commit back?”
Credentials moment: hands up, who was asked for a username and password? Walk through gitcreds::gitcreds_set() in the console with the PAT from pre-work step 4. This is the moment credentials get fixed for everyone, before the afternoon depends on pushing.

title: GitHub for Collaboration
text:
Your turn


Edit your README.md: describe your website in one sentence. Save.
Run the full cycle: Pull, Stage, Commit, Push, with a commit message that says what changed.
Open your repository on github.com and find your commit message and your change.


Place the yellow sticky note on your laptop when you can see your commit on GitHub.








Goal: every learner has at least 2 commits pushed to their own repo by 11:45. The revert stays instructor-only in this block; learners do not revert here.
Bounded regroup: 2 minutes, then park-and-pair; 1:1 fix at lunch.

title: GitHub for Collaboration
text:
Find your partner


Turn to your neighbour: you are now a pair (three is fine if the row runs out).
Write each other’s GitHub username on your sticky notes.
Keep that sticky note. Your partner returns after lunch.



Pairs are formed on the day from who is present; no pre-baked table. The sticky notes with usernames are the pairing record and carry into B6 as reviewer assignments. If someone has no partner, they pair with the helper or the instructor.

title: GitHub for Collaboration
text:
Your turn


Browse to github.com/USERNAME, where USERNAME is your partner’s username.
Clone their website repository (Code button, HTTPS URL, File > New Project > Version Control > Git).
Edit the README: add “This repo contains a personal website for USERNAME.”
Save, stage, commit, and push.

What happens?

Place the yellow sticky note on your laptop when something unexpected happens.








Everyone hits the push wall: 403, no write access. That is the plan; it is the safe-to-fail moment with the lowest stakes of the day. Nobody’s work is harmed, nothing to clean up.
Bounded regroup: 2 minutes, then park-and-pair; 1:1 fix at lunch.

title: GitHub for Collaboration
text:
What just happened?

Your push failed: you can read their repository, but you have no write access.
Giving everyone write access (“collaborator”) exists, but it does not scale, and it means anyone can change anything at any time.
(Forking, making your own copy under your account, exists too; one sentence, not today’s topic.)
So how do teams actually propose and review changes? That is exactly what we solve after lunch: branches and pull requests.


The cliffhanger, stated explicitly. The morning ends with a question the afternoon answers. Do not resolve it now.

title: GitHub for Collaboration
text:
Lunch
Back at 12:55. The afternoon starts hands-on, and your partner is waiting for you.






Hard 12:55 return. B5 starts hands-on as the re-energizer; latecomers can join mid-exercise without missing slides.

title: GitHub for Collaboration
text:
Live drawing: branching
Projector goes to sleep. We draw this one by hand.

Live drawing 3 (branching): the main line splits, commits land on the branch, the merge point stays empty until drawing 4. Under 3 minutes. Fallback: images/drawing-3-branching.png.

title: GitHub for Collaboration
text:
My turn

Watch first; your turn comes next. New repo, new superpower.

In the org, find man-washinvestments-USERNAME and clone it (same moves as this morning).
Git pane: New Branch, name it dev. Read the message about origin out loud.
Open index.qmd, edit the author details (name, ORCID, email, institution), Render.
Switch between main and dev in the Git pane: before the commit nothing differs; after the commit, the change lives only on dev.
Commit “update author details”, push.


Follow along on the screen.



Packages are already installed from the pre-work setup test (its Step 6), so the render takes seconds. Fallback only, if someone’s render fails on a missing package, the commented install chunk sits at the top of index.qmd:
# install.packages(c("devtools", "ggplot2", "ggthemes",
#                    "countrycode", "dplyr", "rmarkdown"))
# devtools::install_github("openwashdata/washinvestments")
Do not run installs for the room; park that learner and pair them.
The branch created here feeds the B6 pull request in 30 minutes, so keep momentum.

title: GitHub for Collaboration
text:
Your turn


Find and clone your man-washinvestments-USERNAME repository (org page, search “man-”).
Git pane: New Branch, name it dev.
In index.qmd: put in your own author details. Render.
Commit “update author details”, push.


Place the yellow sticky note on your laptop when your push has finished.








The render updates docs/index.html, so the commit stages two files together: index.qmd and docs/index.html.
If the roster pivot happened (kill criterion 1), learners without their own repo work in the shared demo repos instead: same steps, repo name from the board. Mark the default before the day.
Bounded regroup: 2 minutes, then park-and-pair. Regroup check before B6: is your branch pushed? Thumbs up. Every learner needs a pushed dev branch with one commit by 13:30.

title: GitHub for Collaboration
text:
Live drawing: the pull request
Projector goes to sleep. We draw this one by hand.

Live drawing 4 (pull request): complete the merge point from drawing 3, pause at the join for the human review, then the lines meet. Under 3 minutes. Fallback: images/drawing-4-pull-request.png.

title: GitHub for Collaboration
text:
My turn

Watch first; your turn comes next. This is the moment the day was built for.

On GitHub: switch to dev, click Compare & pull request. Title: “Add author details to manuscript”.
Request a reviewer. Walk the tabs: Commits, Files changed.
As the reviewer: start a review, leave line comments, submit.
Open an issue with a task list next to the PR.
Merge, confirm, tick off the task in the issue.
Back in RStudio: switch to main, read the message, Pull. Then the gotcha: switch back to dev.


Follow along on the screen.



Demo script from the cis lesson plan. Review comment examples to reuse verbatim: “That’s not a good title for a manuscript. Please suggest 2 alternatives.” and “This list is not complete.”
Read the “behind origin/main, can be fast-forwarded” message out loud when switching to main. The switch-back-to-dev gotcha is a recorded cis learner stumble; name it as a habit: merged? Then pull main, and go back to your working branch.

title: GitHub for Collaboration
text:
Your turn


Open a pull request from your dev branch. Title it so your reviewer knows what changed.
Request a review from your B4 partner (the username on your sticky note).
Review your partner’s pull request: at least one line comment, then submit the review.
After your review arrives: merge your own PR, confirm.
In RStudio: switch to main, Pull, and switch back to dev.

Partner not ready? Review the seed PR in your own repository instead. Nobody waits on anybody.

Place the yellow sticky note on your laptop after the switch back to dev.








Default path: partner-pair review with the B4 sticky-note pairs. Fallback path, on the slide itself: every manuscript repo has a pre-opened seed PR (“Seed: suggestions to review”), so the exercise never blocks on a partner’s progress. If no helper was found by 11 July (kill criterion 4), flip the default: everyone reviews their seed PR, pairs become the bonus.
Target: every learner opens a PR, writes at least one line comment, merges, and pulls, inside 35 minutes.
This block is protected. If the day is behind, B7 Publish is cut, never this. Bounded regroup: 2 minutes, then park-and-pair.

title: GitHub for Collaboration
text:
Our turn

Together, on your merged manuscript:

Open your man-washinvestments-USERNAME repository on GitHub.
Settings > Pages: Deploy from a branch, branch main, folder /docs, Save.
Wait for the address to appear, then paste it into the repository description (About, gear icon).
Open the address: your manuscript is on the web.



First block to cut. If running behind at 14:05, skip to the fallback slide and hand the time to B8. Pages can take a minute to build; use the wait to point at the docs folder in the repo, which is where the rendered manuscript lives.

title: GitHub for Collaboration
text:
Fallback: what publishing looks like

One published example: https://gitforsci-ghe.github.io/website/
The steps, in one breath: Settings, Pages, deploy from branch, docs folder, save.
The website index and the slides you used today are published exactly this way.
Your manuscript repo is ready for it whenever you try, tonight included.


This slide stands alone for the cut case: show it, say the one breath, hand the time to B8.

title: GitHub for Collaboration
text:
Your turn: your map, revisited

Draw a concept map of Git and GitHub as you understand them now.

Use the words from today: repository, clone, commit, push, pull, branch, pull request, merge, review.
Then put it next to your morning map and share both with your partner.


With your consent, I would like to photograph both maps (anonymously) to improve this workshop.








Hard start at 14:15: whatever block is running stops. B8 is protected and runs even on the worst day.
The comparison is the measurement instrument for the whole redesign. Consent is asked on the slide; photograph only the maps of learners who agree, no names on the photos.
Fallback if the morning baseline went badly: draw the Git concept map yourself on the board as the closing recap and have the room call out the verb for each arrow (see the handbook, concept maps as bookends).

title: GitHub for Collaboration
text:
The day in one line
create , clone , commit , push , branch , pull request , review , merge , publish

Recap as verbs, spoken while pointing at the drawings on the board. What you also picked up on the side: RStudio fluency, a first taste of Quarto, and reversibility as a habit.

title: GitHub for Collaboration
text:
What you also learned today

RStudio, now with muscle memory
Quarto: one document, rendered to the web
Reversibility as a habit: commit early, revert without fear

title: GitHub for Collaboration
text:
What comes next

Working with AI coding agents is the follow-up workshop, and it builds on exactly what you did today: branches, pull requests, review.
Interested? Email me and you will hear from me when dates are set.
Start your first own project with Git and GitHub this week (already have a project? Read here).
Keep learning by doing: open pull requests to yourself, review your own diffs.

title: GitHub for Collaboration
text:
Feedback, and thanks!

Please fill in the post-course survey (link shared by email, 5 minutes, anonymous).
Email me your open questions; I answer all of them this week.

All material is licensed under Creative Commons Attribution Share Alike 4.0 International.

Survey link goes out by email right now, while the slide is up, so phones can grab it. End on momentum: recap done, next step named, thanks, done by 14:30.

title: GitHub for Collaboration
text:
Thanks!
Slides created via revealjs and Quarto: https://quarto.org/docs/presentations/revealjs/
Access slides as PDF on GitHub
All material is licensed under Creative Commons Attribution Share Alike 4.0 International.

title: GitHub for Collaboration
text: References

title: Why Git? Why GitHub?
text:
The book Happy Git and GitHub for the useR is a great resource for learning about Git and GitHub. Today you have worked through almost all elements of the first 14 chapters of the book. I am taking a lot of resources from the book itself and highly recommend it as a reference for you.
Before we start our workshop on Wednesday, it is important to learn about the big picture of Git and GitHub. This will help you understand why we are using these tools and how they fit into the larger context of data science and research.

Read the chapter, “Why Git? Why GitHub?” https://happygitwithr.com/big-picture
Take notes and write down questions. Bring them to the workshop, or open an issue on your 01-setup-USERNAME repository and assign it to larnsce (the same way you did in Step 6c). That way I can answer before we meet.

Reading this chapter beforehand lets me focus on the practical aspects of Git and GitHub during the workshop, rather than spending time on the theory. I will assume you have read it and understand the big picture of why we are using these tools.

title: Test your setup (2 of 3): Configure Git and push
text:
This is the second of three short steps. Your setup repository is now open in RStudio (from Step 6a). Here you store your GitHub token, introduce yourself to Git, and push your first change to GitHub.
The repository contains three numbered R scripts. You run them in order by opening a script and clicking the Source button in the top-right corner of the script window. This step covers 01-github-token.R and 02-git-configuration.R; the next step covers 03-workshop-packages.R.

title: Test your setup (2 of 3): Configure Git and push
text:
Step 1: Store your GitHub token

In the Files tab (bottom-right pane), click on 01-github-token.R to open it in the editor.
Have the GitHub Personal Access Token (PAT) ready that you created in Step 4 of the pre-course work. If you no longer have it, create a new token on GitHub first.
Click the Source button in the top-right corner of the script window.



When the Console asks for the token, paste it and press Enter.







NoteYou may have stored a token already



If you already stored a token on this laptop earlier, the Console does not ask you to paste a new one. Instead it shows a numbered menu, as in the screenshot below. Updating the token is up to you: choose “Replace these credentials” to store a new one, or “Abort update” to keep the existing token.









ImportantPaste the token into the Console, never into the file



Do not type or paste your token into the script file. Paste it only into the Console when asked. This keeps your token out of the repository.

title: Test your setup (2 of 3): Configure Git and push
text:
Step 2: Introduce yourself to Git

In the Files tab, click on 02-git-configuration.R to open it in the editor.
In Step 1 of the script, replace the two placeholders. Keep the quotation marks.

your_name is your full name, for example "Ada Lovelace" (not your GitHub username)
your_email is the email address you use for your GitHub account

Click the Source button in the top-right corner of the script window. It runs the whole script.

title: Test your setup (2 of 3): Configure Git and push
section: Step 3: Commit and push your changes
text:
Step 3: Commit and push your changes

Navigate to the Git pane in the top-right window of RStudio.



Check the box next to 02-git-configuration.R to stage it for commit.



Click on the “Commit” button.








Note



A new window will open. In the top left corner, you will see the files that you have staged for commit. In the bottom windows, you can see the changes you made in the file. The top right corner shows the “Commit message” which cannot be blank. Each Commit requires a Commit message.



Enter a commit message in the “Commit message” field. For example: “complete git configuration”



Click on the “Commit” button.



Close the window that pops up.



Click on the “Push” button.







Note



Above the top left window, you will now see the sentence “Your branch is ahead of ‘origin/main’ by 1 commit”. This means that you have made changes to your local repository that are not yet pushed to GitHub. It also means that your Commit was successful.




Does the pop window say HEAD -> main as the final statement? Close it. If not, copy what it says and email it to me now.



Verify that the changes have been pushed to GitHub by refreshing the page for your repository 01-setup-USERNAME in your browser (for example, 01-setup-rainbow-train). You should see the commit message you just wrote next to 02-git-configuration.R.

TODO: screenshot of the GitHub repository page after push (to be added).

title: Test your setup (2 of 3): Configure Git and push
text:
Next step
You have configured Git and pushed your first change. Continue with Step 6c: Install the workshop packages and report done.

title: Download and install Git
text:
Tip



If you run into problems with this task, contact me by email: lschoebitz@ethz.ch




Install Git for Windows after download from this direct download link: https://github.com/git-for-windows/git/releases/download/v2.49.0.windows.1/Git-2.49.0-64-bit.exe
Git for Windows is also known as msysgit or “Git Bash”, to get Git in addition to some other useful tools, such as the Bash shell. Yes, all those names are totally confusing, but you might encounter them elsewhere and I want you to be well-informed.
We like this because Git for Windows leaves the Git executable in a conventional location, which will help you and other programs, e.g. RStudio, find it and use it. This also supports a transition to more expert use, because the “Git Bash” shell will be useful as you venture outside of R/RStudio.

NOTE: When asked about “Adjusting your PATH environment”, make sure to select “Git from the command line and also from 3rd-party software”. Otherwise, we believe it is good to accept the defaults.
Note that RStudio for Windows prefers for Git to be installed below C:/Program Files and this appears to be the default. This implies, for example, that the Git executable on my Windows system is found at C:/Program Files/Git/bin/git.exe. Unless you have specific reasons to otherwise, follow this convention.




It’s slightly more complicated. Contact me if you have questions.
Option 1 (highly recommended): Install the Xcode command line tools (not all of Xcode), which includes Git.

Open the Terminal application on Mac and type xcode-select --install. If you don’t have it installed, you will be prompted to install the Xcode command line tools. Follow the instructions.

Option 2 (recommended): If you anticipate getting heavily into scientific computing, you’re going to be installing and updating lots of software. You should check out Homebrew, “the missing package manager for OS X”. Among many other things, it can install Git for you.

Open the Terminal application on Mac, copy and run the following line:

/bin/bash -c "$(curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Homebrew/install/HEAD/install.sh)"
This will install Homebrew. You may be prompted for your password. If you are, type it in and hit enter. You won’t see anything appear on the screen as you type, but it is working.
Once you have Homebrew installed, type and run this in the terminal.
brew install git
We will confirm in Test your setup that you have Git installed and working.

title: Download and install Git
text:
Install Git for Windows after download from this direct download link: https://github.com/git-for-windows/git/releases/download/v2.49.0.windows.1/Git-2.49.0-64-bit.exe
Git for Windows is also known as msysgit or “Git Bash”, to get Git in addition to some other useful tools, such as the Bash shell. Yes, all those names are totally confusing, but you might encounter them elsewhere and I want you to be well-informed.
We like this because Git for Windows leaves the Git executable in a conventional location, which will help you and other programs, e.g. RStudio, find it and use it. This also supports a transition to more expert use, because the “Git Bash” shell will be useful as you venture outside of R/RStudio.

This implies, for example, that the Git executable on my Windows system is found at C:/Program Files/Git/bin/git.exe. Unless you have specific reasons to otherwise, follow this convention.

It’s slightly more complicated. Contact me if you have questions.
Option 1 (highly recommended): Install the Xcode command line tools (not all of Xcode), which includes Git.

Open the Terminal application on Mac and type xcode-select --install. If you don’t have it installed, you will be prompted to install the Xcode command line tools. Follow the instructions.

Option 2 (recommended): If you anticipate getting heavily into scientific computing, you’re going to be installing and updating lots of software. You should check out Homebrew, “the missing package manager for OS X”. Among many other things, it can install Git for you.

Open the Terminal application on Mac, copy and run the following line:

/bin/bash -c "$(curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Homebrew/install/HEAD/install.sh)"
This will install Homebrew. You may be prompted for your password. If you are, type it in and hit enter. You won’t see anything appear on the screen as you type, but it is working.
Once you have Homebrew installed, type and run this in the terminal.

title: Git for Scientists (Summer 2026)
text:
At this point, we need your GitHub username. We have also developed this survey to get an idea about your prior knowledge. It will help us shape the course to ensure that everyone gets an opportunity to learn at their pace.

Complete the survey by end of Thursday, 09 July 2026: https://forms.gle/iNoMpzModYpY3iAn8

Complete the survey by end of Thursday, 09 July 2026: https://forms.gle/iNoMpzModYpY3iAn8

title: Git for Scientists (Summer 2026)
text:
ImportantDo you already have a R version of R and RStudio?





Even if you already have R and RStudio installed, it is strongly recommended to download and install the latest versions to ensure compatibility with Quarto and access to the newest features and updates. Up-to-date installations help avoid unexpected errors and ensure all features work as intended.




R for Windows: https://cran.rstudio.com/bin/windows/
R for Mac: https://cran.rstudio.com/bin/macosx/ (Note: Two separate downloads for Apple silicon M1, M2, etc. Macs and Intel Macs)
RStudio: https://posit.co/download/rstudio-desktop/ for your operating system (Windows or Mac)
Follow your operating system’s installation process

title: Git for Scientists (Summer 2026)
text:
ImportantDo you already have a R version of R and RStudio?





Even if you already have R and RStudio installed, it is strongly recommended to download and install the latest versions to ensure compatibility with Quarto and access to the newest features and updates. Up-to-date installations help avoid unexpected errors and ensure all features work as intended.




R for Windows: https://cran.rstudio.com/bin/windows/
R for Mac: https://cran.rstudio.com/bin/macosx/ (Note: Two separate downloads for Apple silicon M1, M2, etc. Macs and Intel Macs)
RStudio: https://posit.co/download/rstudio-desktop/ for your operating system (Windows or Mac)
Follow your operating system’s installation process

title: Pre-course work: Git for Scientists
text:
Thank you for your interest in this course. Your course instructor Lars Schöbitz is looking forward to meet you.
We will meet on Wednesday, 15 July 2026 - 09:30 to 14:30 (Europe/Zurich) at ETH Zurich, in the CLD Building, Raum A1. Instructions to find us: https://ghe.ethz.ch/about/how-to-find-us.html
Registration for this workshop is closed. You can still share your interest in a future iteration through this form: https://forms.gle/Zf5fPsPJqUxEoLgKA

title: Pre-course work: Git for Scientists
text:
Background
AI coding agents are rapidly entering scientific work, and using them with confidence starts with the same collaboration skills that make any research project reproducible and reviewable. This workshop gives you those foundations. You will learn to use Git and GitHub for collaboration in scientific work, so that later you can treat an AI agent as a collaborator whose every change is versioned, documented, and open to review. It is the first step in getting ready for AI-assisted scientific work.

title: Pre-course work: Git for Scientists
text:
Prior knowledge
For this course, we assume that you have some prior knowledge of R and RStudio. You should be able to:

install and load R packages
read and run short R scripts
edit and run R Markdown / Quarto documents
navigate the RStudio IDE
identify the four panes of the RStudio IDE (Script, Environment, Files, Console)

title: Pre-course work: Git for Scientists
section: What do I need to bring to the workshop?
text:
What do I need to bring to the workshop?
Please ensure you have the following available:

a laptop, connected to wifi
a web browser (preferably Chrome or Firefox)
a pen and paper (we will be doing some drawings)
a completed list of pre-work tasks with intermediate due dates

Prior to the workshop, there is some pre-work for you to do. This pre-work will ensure that we can focus our time together on building collaboration skills instead of setting up infrastructure.

title: Pre-course work: Git for Scientists
section: What do I need to prepare before the workshop?
text:
What do I need to prepare before the workshop?
Prior to the workshop, complete the following seven steps:

Step 1: Download and install R and RStudio
Step 2: Get an account for GitHub
Step 3: Complete the pre-course survey by end of Thursday, 09 July 2026.
Step 4: Create a personal access token for GitHub
Step 5: Install Git
Step 6: Check your complete setup by Monday, 13 July 2026, in three short parts (unlocked after the pre-course survey is completed):

Step 6a: Clone the setup repository
Step 6b: Configure Git and push
Step 6c: Install packages and report done

Step 7: Read “Why Git? Why GitHub?” from “Happy Git and GitHub for the useR”!

title: Pre-course work: Git for Scientists
text:
Schedule









Time
Module




09:30 - 10:00
Arrival and setup check


10:00 - 10:30
B1: Welcome, concept-map intro, baseline map


10:30 - 11:00
B2: Create and clone (website repo)


11:00 - 11:15
Break


11:15 - 11:45
B3: The commit cycle (website repo)


11:45 - 12:15
B4: Collaboration cliffhanger (partner clone, push fails)


12:15 - 13:00
Lunch


13:00 - 13:30
B5: Branch (manuscript repo)


13:30 - 14:05
B6: Pull request, review, merge


14:05 - 14:15
B7: Publish


14:15 - 14:30
B8: Concept map revisit and wrap-up

title: Pre-course work: Git for Scientists
text:
Learning objectives
By the end of this workshop, participants will be able to:

Create and clone a repository, and apply the standard Git workflow from the RStudio IDE: pulling, staging, committing, and pushing changes, including reverting a commit.
Work with branches and pull requests: create a development branch, open a pull request, review and comment on a colleague’s pull request, and merge after review.
Publish a rendered document via GitHub Pages, and draw a concept map of how Git and GitHub fit together as a foundation for collaboration.

title: Pre-course work: Git for Scientists
text:
Thanks!
Thank you for working through these steps. We are looking forward to meeting you at the workshop.

title: Git for Scientists (Summer 2026)
text:
To set up an account on GitHub, follow these general steps:

Go to the GitHub website at https://github.com/.
Click on the “Sign up” button on the top right corner of the page.
Enter your preferred username, email address, and password in the provided fields.
Choose whether you want to receive updates and announcements via email.
Complete the verification process by solving a puzzle and entering a verification code sent to your email.







TipUsername advice from: Happy Git and GitHub for the useR





You will be able to upgrade to a paid level of service, apply discounts, join organizations, etc. in the future, so don’t fret about any of that now. Except your username. You might want to give that some thought.
A few tips, which sadly tend to contradict each other:

Incorporate your actual name! People like to know who they’re dealing with. Also makes your username easier for people to guess or remember.
Reuse your username from other contexts, e.g., Twitter or Slack. But, of course, someone with no GitHub activity will probably be squatting on that.
Pick a username you will be comfortable revealing to your future boss.
Shorter is better than longer.
Be as unique as possible in as few characters as possible. In some settings GitHub auto-completes or suggests usernames.
Make it timeless. Don’t highlight your current university, employer, or place of residence, e.g. JennyFromTheBlock.
Avoid words laden with special meaning in programming.
Avoid the use of upper vs. lower case to separate words. We highly recommend all lowercase. GitHub treats usernames in a case insensitive way, but using all lowercase is kinder to people doing downstream regular expression work with usernames, in various languages. A better strategy for word separation is to use a hyphen -.

You can change your username later, but better to get this right the first time.

https://help.github.com/articles/changing-your-github-username/
https://help.github.com/articles/what-happens-when-i-change-my-username/




Once you have created your GitHub Account, please fill out the pre-course survey immediately. We will then invite you to join the GitHub Organisation for the course.

title: Git for Scientists (Summer 2026)
text:
To set up an account on GitHub, follow these general steps:

Go to the GitHub website at https://github.com/.
Click on the “Sign up” button on the top right corner of the page.
Enter your preferred username, email address, and password in the provided fields.
Choose whether you want to receive updates and announcements via email.
Complete the verification process by solving a puzzle and entering a verification code sent to your email.







TipUsername advice from: Happy Git and GitHub for the useR





You will be able to upgrade to a paid level of service, apply discounts, join organizations, etc. in the future, so don’t fret about any of that now. Except your username. You might want to give that some thought.
A few tips, which sadly tend to contradict each other:

Incorporate your actual name! People like to know who they’re dealing with. Also makes your username easier for people to guess or remember.
Reuse your username from other contexts, e.g., Twitter or Slack. But, of course, someone with no GitHub activity will probably be squatting on that.
Pick a username you will be comfortable revealing to your future boss.
Shorter is better than longer.
Be as unique as possible in as few characters as possible. In some settings GitHub auto-completes or suggests usernames.
Make it timeless. Don’t highlight your current university, employer, or place of residence, e.g. JennyFromTheBlock.
Avoid words laden with special meaning in programming.
Avoid the use of upper vs. lower case to separate words. We highly recommend all lowercase. GitHub treats usernames in a case insensitive way, but using all lowercase is kinder to people doing downstream regular expression work with usernames, in various languages. A better strategy for word separation is to use a hyphen -.

You can change your username later, but better to get this right the first time.

https://help.github.com/articles/changing-your-github-username/
https://help.github.com/articles/what-happens-when-i-change-my-username/




Once you have created your GitHub Account, please fill out the pre-course survey immediately. We will then invite you to join the GitHub Organisation for the course.

title: Create a GitHub Personal Access Token
text:
Create a GitHub Personal Access Token
Here are the step-by-step instructions to set up a GitHub personal access token (PAT):






Important



Treat your access tokens like passwords and keep them secure (e.g. a password managing software).



Log in to your GitHub account.
Click on your profile picture in the top-right corner of the page.
Select “Settings” from the dropdown menu.
In the left sidebar, click on “Developer settings”.
Click on “Personal access tokens”.
Click on “Tokens (classic)”.
Click on “Generate new token” and from the dropdown menu select “Generate new token (classic)”.
In the “Note” field, give your token the name: GITHUB_PAT
Under Expiration, select “No expiration”
Under “Select scopes”, select: repo, workflow, user
Click on “Generate token”.
Your personal access token will be displayed on the screen. Copy it and store it in a safe place.







Tip



You will need this token in your pre-course work and throughout the workshop.

title: Test your setup (1 of 3): Clone the setup repository
text: This is the first of three short steps that test whether your setup is complete and working. In this step, you accept your invitation to the GitHub organization and clone your personal setup repository into RStudio.

title: Test your setup (1 of 3): Clone the setup repository
text:
Step 1: Accept invitation from GitHub

Open your email that you used to register for your GitHub account.
Find the email from GitHub with the subject “[GitHub] @larnsce invited you to join the ‘gitforsci-ghe’ organization”.
Click on “Join @gitforsci-ghe” link in the email to accept the invitation and work through the steps that follow in your browser to accept the invitation.

title: Test your setup (1 of 3): Clone the setup repository
text:
Step 2: Create a bookmark

Open the landing page for the GitHub organization ‘gitforsci-ghe’: https://github.com/gitforsci-ghe



Bookmark this page in your browser.



Organize the bookmark into a folder called “gitforsci-ghe” or similar.



Use this folder to add other bookmarks that are relevant for this workshop (e.g. the workshop website)

title: Test your setup (1 of 3): Clone the setup repository
text:
Step 3: Clone the setup repository

Open the landing page for the GitHub organization ‘gitforsci-ghe’: https://github.com/gitforsci-ghe (try to use your bookmark)
Navigate down the page to the section “Repositories” and use the search field to find the repository called 01-setup-USERNAME where USERNAME is your GitHub username.



Click on the repository name to open it.







ImportantNo repository with your username?



Contact me by email if your repository is not there. I will create it for you.




Click on the green “Code” button.



Copy the HTTPS URL to your clipboard by clicking on the clipboard sign.



Open the RStudio IDE on your laptop.







Note



I am working on MacOS, but the steps should be similar on Windows.




Click on File > New Project in the top menu bar



Click on Version Control.



Click on Git.



Click on Browse… button.



Create a folder structure for Git repositories (e.g. ~/Documents/gitrepos/gh-org-gitforsci-ghe)







NoteFolder structure for Git repositories and naming conventions



I highly recommend storing folders that are Git repositories separate from other folders. This will help you to keep track of your Git repositories and avoid confusion with other folders.
Create this directory structure locally on your laptop and not in folders that are synced with cloud storage (e.g. Google Drive, Dropbox, OneDrive, etc.).
In your directory structure, you can create a folder called gitrepos. Inside this folder, you can create subfolders for each GitHub organization you are working with (e.g. gh-org-gitforsci-ghe). A subfolder for your personal profile would be gh-rainbow-train. If you also work with GitLab or other Git hosting platforms, use the abbreviation for the platform in the folder name (e.g. gitrepos/gh-rainbow-train, gitrepos/gl-rainbow-train, etc.).
This strategy will help you to keep track of your Git repositories and the respective platforms and organisations they belong to.



Paste the HTTPS URL from GitHub into the “Repository URL” field.







NoteThe “Project directory name” field



After you paste the URL, RStudio usually fills in the “Project directory name” field for you. If it stays empty, leave it empty and do not type anything. Creating the project works with an empty field.




Click on the “Create Project” button.



Find the Files tab in the bottom right pane of RStudio. You will see three numbered R scripts: 01-github-token.R, 02-git-configuration.R, and 03-workshop-packages.R. You will work through them in the next two steps.

title: Test your setup (1 of 3): Clone the setup repository
text:
Next step
Your repository is cloned and open in RStudio. Continue with Step 6b: Configure Git and push your first change.

title: Test your setup (3 of 3): Install packages and report done
text: This is the last of three short steps. Your setup repository is open in RStudio and Git is configured (from Step 6b). Here you install the workshop packages and open an issue so I can verify your setup.

title: Test your setup (3 of 3): Install packages and report done
text:
Step 1: Install the workshop packages
In the workshop, you will work on a small manuscript that uses the washinvestments R data package. Installing the packages now means the manuscript renders in under a minute on the day, and we can spend our time together on Git and GitHub instead of watching progress bars.

In the Files tab (bottom-right pane), click on 03-workshop-packages.R to open it in the editor. There is nothing to edit in this script.
Click the Source button in the top-right corner of the script window. Installing takes a few minutes.
Wait for the installation to finish. If the Console asks a question about updating packages, press Enter.
Check the Console for the line All workshop packages installed.







ImportantError message instead?



Copy the full error message and email it to me. Getting this resolved before the workshop keeps your afternoon free for the interesting parts.

title: Test your setup (3 of 3): Install packages and report done
section: Step 2: Open an issue on GitHub
text:
Step 2: Open an issue on GitHub

Open the landing page for the GitHub organization ‘gitforsci-ghe’: https://github.com/gitforsci-ghe (try to use your bookmark)
Navigate down the page to the section “Repositories” and use the search field to find the repository called 01-setup-USERNAME where USERNAME is your GitHub username.
Click on the repository name to open it.
Click on the “Issues” tab.



Click on the green “New issue” button.



In the “Add a title” field, write: “Completed setup tasks”. You can add a short description too, for example a question you would like me to look at.



On the right, click the gear next to “Assignees” and select the course instructor larnsce.



Confirm that larnsce now appears in the Assignees field, then click the green “Create” button. Assigning me the issue notifies me the same way a mention does; treat an assigned issue as a task you hand to me.



Your issue is created. It is now open and assigned to larnsce.


Congratulations! Your setup is complete and you have mastered the key steps of using Git and GitHub. Now onto some important reading and I will see you in class.
